body_txt: Amanojaku (天邪鬼) is a type of Oni characterized by their physical might, vileness, and manipulative nature. Its appearances in Japanese folklore vary, but it consistently acts in opposition to morality, order, and self-interest. At times, it acts as a corrupting force. It employs a variety of tactics, such as mind-reading, possession , and impersonation (dressing up in stolen clothes, disguising itself on a murdered human's skin, and magically mimicking others' voices). Modern depictions of the Amanojaku show it to be obsessed with contrariness to the point of predictability , making it easy to beat them via Reverse Psychology . For example, if accosted by one, telling the oni how happy one is with its company will cause it to vanish. In youkai-centric works, amanojaku are usually prey to self-contradiction, preventing them from acting consistently or in accordance with social expectations. Amanojaku has roots in Shinto . Its most commonly accepted origin (found in the Kojiki and the Nihon Shoki ) links it to the vassal kami Amenosagume, who misinterpreted the gods' pheasant messenger to be a bad omen, therefore causing Earth's ruler Amewakahiko’s death when he angered the gods by shooting it. Other legends connect the amanojaku with Amanozako, an obnoxious goddess regarded as the ancestress of wicked youkai, including the Tengu . Fittingly, "zako" (ざこ) translates as "opposing all". In Japanese Buddhism iconography, amanojaku symbolize wickedness and appear as defeated, monstrous creatures beneath sculptures of Bishamonten and the Four Heavenly Kings. Alternatively, the amanojaku appear as faces on their armor. It's assumed this role is due to syncretism with the early Buddhist interpretation of the yaksha. As a result ot the above, this youkai's etymology is fairly complicated. "Amanojaku" translates as "heavenly evil oni" and can be decomposed into "ama no" (which references Amenosagume, Amanozako, and contrariness) and "jaki" (linked with evil and power). Related to the latter is "akki", a wicked oni. All three terms broadly denote oni trampled by the Four Heavenly Kings, further muddying distinctions between classic amanojaku and other oni (e.g., the lantern-carrying Tentōki and Ryūtōki niō; Niranba and Biranba of the Ten Rakshasis). In Japanese folklore, the Amanojaku is a key character in the legends that explain why some plants have red roots (seen in Urikohime and the Amanojaku ) or the reason behind the peculiar shapes of rock formations (seen in Atmanojaku no Kasaneiwa ). Sub-Trope of Always Chaotic Evil . It might be a Consummate Liar , and think Bad Is Good and Good Is Bad . See also The Corrupter and Commander Contrarian . Some interpretations do away with the oni's physical strength and portray it as The Imp .
